Home › Cheat Sheets › Streaming Algorithms

Streaming Algorithms

Last updated: Feb 28, 2026

Table of Contents

  • 0) Concept
  • 0-0) Why Streaming Algorithms?
  • 0-1) Types
  • 0-2) When to Use Each Algorithm
  • 1) Algorithm Templates
  • 1-1) Reservoir Sampling (LC 382, 398)
  • 1-2) Boyer-Moore Majority Vote (LC 169, 229)
  • 1-3) Count-Min Sketch - Frequency Estimation
  • 1-4) Bloom Filter - Membership Testing
  • 2) LeetCode Problems by Pattern
  • 2-1) Reservoir Sampling
  • 2-2) Boyer-Moore Majority Vote
  • 2-3) Count-Min Sketch (Conceptual)
  • 2-4) Bloom Filter (Conceptual)
  • 2-5) Related Streaming Problems
  • 3) Common Mistakes & Edge Cases
  • 🚫 Common Mistakes
  • ⚠️ Edge Cases
  • 4) Interview Tips & Complexity Analysis
  • 💡 Interview Strategy
  • 📊 Complexity Analysis
  • 🎯 Interview Talking Points
  • 🔧 Optimization Techniques
  • 📚 Related Topics
  • 5) Complete Code Examples
  • Example 1: Weighted Reservoir Sampling (LC 528)
  • Example 2: Stream Median (Related Problem LC 295)
  • Summary

Streaming Algorithms

  • Process data streams with limited memory
  • When to use: Data too large to store, online processing, random sampling, frequency estimation
  • Key LeetCode problems: LC 382, 398 (Reservoir Sampling), LC 169, 229 (Majority Element), LC 528 (Random Pick with Weight)
  • Core techniques: Reservoir sampling, probabilistic data structures, approximate counting
  • Space-time tradeoff: Trade accuracy for memory efficiency

Time Complexity: O(1) per element processed (streaming) Space Complexity: O(k) or O(log n) typically, independent of stream size

0) Concept

0-0) Why Streaming Algorithms?

Problem: Traditional algorithms assume entire dataset fits in memory

  • Streaming Context: Data arrives continuously, too large to store
  • Memory Constraint: Limited space, must process elements once
  • Requirements: Make decisions with incomplete information

Key Characteristics:

  • Single Pass: Process each element at most once
  • Sublinear Space: Memory << data size
  • Approximate Results: Trade exactness for efficiency (except Reservoir Sampling)
  • Online Processing: Results available anytime

0-1) Types

1. Reservoir Sampling - Uniform Random Sampling

  • Purpose: Select k random elements from stream of unknown size
  • Guarantee: Each element has exactly k/n probability of selection
  • Space: O(k) for k samples
  • Applications: Random sampling, shuffling, fairness in selection

2. Morris Counter - Approximate Counting

  • Purpose: Count large numbers with small memory
  • Space: O(log log n) instead of O(log n)
  • Trade-off: Approximate count with expected value n
  • Applications: Large-scale counting, memory-constrained systems

3. Boyer-Moore Majority Vote - Heavy Hitters

  • Purpose: Find element(s) appearing > n/k times
  • Space: O(k) for top-k elements
  • Applications: Majority element, top-k frequent elements
  • Variants: Standard (k=2), Generalized (arbitrary k)

4. Count-Min Sketch - Frequency Estimation

  • Purpose: Estimate frequency of elements in stream
  • Space: O(w × d) hash table, w×d << n
  • Guarantee: Overestimate by at most εn with probability 1-δ
  • Applications: Network traffic analysis, query frequency

5. Bloom Filter - Membership Testing

  • Purpose: Test if element is in set (probabilistic)
  • Space: O(m) bits, m << n
  • Guarantee: No false negatives, false positives with probability p
  • Applications: Cache filtering, spell checking, malware detection

0-2) When to Use Each Algorithm

Algorithm Use Case Space Accuracy Output
Reservoir Sampling Random k samples O(k) Exact k elements
Morris Counter Count stream size O(log log n) Approximate ~count
Boyer-Moore Majority element O(k) Exact if exists Candidates
Count-Min Sketch Frequency estimation O(w×d) Approximate ~frequency
Bloom Filter Set membership O(m) False positives yes/no

Recognition Keywords:

  • “Random sample”, “shuffle”, “fairness” → Reservoir Sampling
  • “Majority element”, “appears more than n/k times” → Boyer-Moore
  • “Estimate frequency”, “approximate count” → Count-Min Sketch
  • “Check membership”, “seen before?” → Bloom Filter
  • “Limited memory”, “one pass”, “streaming data” → Any streaming algorithm

1) Algorithm Templates

1-1) Reservoir Sampling (LC 382, 398)

Algorithm Overview:

  • Maintain reservoir of k elements
  • For element at index i (i ≥ k):
    • Generate random j ∈ [0, i]
    • If j < k, replace reservoir[j] with current element
  • Proof: Element at index i has k/i chance to enter, k/(i+1) to stay → k/n final probability

Template 1: Single Random Element (k=1)

1// LC 382 - Linked List Random Node
2class Solution {
3    private ListNode head;
4    private Random random;
5
6    public Solution(ListNode head) {
7        this.head = head;
8        this.random = new Random();
9    }
10
11    /**
12     * time = O(N)
13     * space = O(1)
14     */
15    public int getRandom() {
16        ListNode cur = head;
17        int result = cur.val;
18        int count = 1;
19
20        // Process stream: for each element i, include with prob 1/i
21        while (cur != null) {
22            // Generate random in [0, count-1], replace if 0
23            if (random.nextInt(count) == 0) {
24                result = cur.val;
25            }
26            count++;
27            cur = cur.next;
28        }
29        return result;
30    }
31}

1# Python implementation
2import random
3
4class Solution:
5    def __init__(self, head: ListNode):
6        self.head = head
7
8    def getRandom(self) -> int:
9        cur = self.head
10        result = cur.val
11        count = 1
12
13        cur = cur.next
14        while cur:
15            count += 1
16            # Include current element with probability 1/count
17            if random.randint(1, count) == count:
18                result = cur.val
19            cur = cur.next
20
21        return result

Template 2: k Random Elements

1// LC 398 - Random Pick Index (variant: pick from array with duplicates)
2class Solution {
3    private int[] nums;
4    private Random random;
5
6    public Solution(int[] nums) {
7        this.nums = nums;
8        this.random = new Random();
9    }
10
11    /**
12     * time = O(N)
13     * space = O(1)
14     */
15    public int pick(int target) {
16        int result = -1;
17        int count = 0;
18
19        for (int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
20            if (nums[i] == target) {
21                count++;
22                // Replace with probability 1/count
23                if (random.nextInt(count) == 0) {
24                    result = i;
25                }
26            }
27        }
28        return result;
29    }
30}

Template 3: General k-Reservoir Sampling

1class ReservoirSampling {
2    private int k;
3    private int[] reservoir;
4    private Random random;
5    private int count; // Elements seen so far
6
7    public ReservoirSampling(int k) {
8        this.k = k;
9        this.reservoir = new int[k];
10        this.random = new Random();
11        this.count = 0;
12    }
13
14    /**
15     * time = O(1) per element
16     * space = O(k)
17     */
18    public void add(int val) {
19        if (count < k) {
20            // Fill reservoir first
21            reservoir[count] = val;
22        } else {
23            // Generate random index in [0, count]
24            int j = random.nextInt(count + 1);
25            if (j < k) {
26                // Replace element in reservoir
27                reservoir[j] = val;
28            }
29        }
30        count++;
31    }
32
33    public int[] getSample() {
34        return Arrays.copyOf(reservoir, Math.min(k, count));
35    }
36}

1# Python k-reservoir sampling
2import random
3
4class ReservoirSampling:
5    def __init__(self, k: int):
6        self.k = k
7        self.reservoir = []
8        self.count = 0
9
10    def add(self, val: int) -> None:
11        """Time: O(1), Space: O(k)"""
12        if self.count < self.k:
13            self.reservoir.append(val)
14        else:
15            # Random index in [0, count]
16            j = random.randint(0, self.count)
17            if j < self.k:
18                self.reservoir[j] = val
19        self.count += 1
20
21    def get_sample(self) -> list:
22        return self.reservoir[:]

Key Insight: Probability math ensures uniform distribution

  • Element i enters with probability k/i
  • Stays with probability: (1 - k/(i+1)) × (1 - k/(i+2)) × … × (1 - k/n)
  • Final probability: k/i × i/(i+1) × (i+1)/(i+2) × … × (n-1)/n = k/n ✓

1-2) Boyer-Moore Majority Vote (LC 169, 229)

Algorithm Overview:

  • Find element(s) appearing more than ⌊n/k⌋ times
  • Key Idea: Pair different elements and cancel them out
  • Majority element will survive cancellation
  • Two-phase: (1) Find candidates, (2) Verify counts

Template 1: Standard Majority Element (> n/2)

1// LC 169 - Majority Element
2class Solution {
3    /**
4     * time = O(N)
5     * space = O(1)
6     */
7    public int majorityElement(int[] nums) {
8        int candidate = nums[0];
9        int count = 1;
10
11        // Phase 1: Find candidate
12        for (int i = 1; i < nums.length; i++) {
13            if (count == 0) {
14                candidate = nums[i];
15                count = 1;
16            } else if (nums[i] == candidate) {
17                count++;
18            } else {
19                count--; // Cancel out different element
20            }
21        }
22
23        // Phase 2: Verify (not needed if majority guaranteed)
24        // If not guaranteed, count candidate appearances
25        return candidate;
26    }
27}

1# Python implementation
2def majorityElement(nums: list[int]) -> int:
3    """Time: O(n), Space: O(1)"""
4    candidate = None
5    count = 0
6
7    # Phase 1: Find candidate
8    for num in nums:
9        if count == 0:
10            candidate = num
11            count = 1
12        elif num == candidate:
13            count += 1
14        else:
15            count -= 1
16
17    return candidate

Template 2: Generalized - Elements Appearing > n/3 Times (LC 229)

1// LC 229 - Majority Element II (> n/3)
2class Solution {
3    /**
4     * time = O(N)
5     * space = O(1)
6     */
7    public List<Integer> majorityElement(int[] nums) {
8        // At most 2 elements can appear > n/3 times
9        int candidate1 = 0, candidate2 = 0;
10        int count1 = 0, count2 = 0;
11
12        // Phase 1: Find candidates (at most k-1 candidates for n/k)
13        for (int num : nums) {
14            if (num == candidate1) {
15                count1++;
16            } else if (num == candidate2) {
17                count2++;
18            } else if (count1 == 0) {
19                candidate1 = num;
20                count1 = 1;
21            } else if (count2 == 0) {
22                candidate2 = num;
23                count2 = 1;
24            } else {
25                // Different from both: cancel out
26                count1--;
27                count2--;
28            }
29        }
30
31        // Phase 2: Verify candidates
32        count1 = 0;
33        count2 = 0;
34        for (int num : nums) {
35            if (num == candidate1) count1++;
36            else if (num == candidate2) count2++;
37        }
38
39        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
40        if (count1 > nums.length / 3) result.add(candidate1);
41        if (count2 > nums.length / 3) result.add(candidate2);
42
43        return result;
44    }
45}

1# Python - Majority Element II
2def majorityElement(nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
3    """Time: O(n), Space: O(1)"""
4    # Phase 1: Find up to 2 candidates
5    candidate1, candidate2 = None, None
6    count1, count2 = 0, 0
7
8    for num in nums:
9        if num == candidate1:
10            count1 += 1
11        elif num == candidate2:
12            count2 += 1
13        elif count1 == 0:
14            candidate1, count1 = num, 1
15        elif count2 == 0:
16            candidate2, count2 = num, 1
17        else:
18            count1 -= 1
19            count2 -= 1
20
21    # Phase 2: Verify
22    result = []
23    for candidate in [candidate1, candidate2]:
24        if nums.count(candidate) > len(nums) // 3:
25            result.append(candidate)
26
27    return result

Generalized Template: Top k-1 Elements (> n/k)

1class BoyerMooreGeneralized {
2    /**
3     * Find elements appearing > n/k times
4     * time = O(N * k)
5     * space = O(k)
6     */
7    public List<Integer> majorityElement(int[] nums, int k) {
8        // Can have at most k-1 elements appearing > n/k times
9        Map<Integer, Integer> candidates = new HashMap<>();
10
11        // Phase 1: Find candidates
12        for (int num : nums) {
13            if (candidates.containsKey(num)) {
14                candidates.put(num, candidates.get(num) + 1);
15            } else if (candidates.size() < k - 1) {
16                candidates.put(num, 1);
17            } else {
18                // Decrement all counts (cancellation)
19                List<Integer> toRemove = new ArrayList<>();
20                for (Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> entry : candidates.entrySet()) {
21                    int count = entry.getValue() - 1;
22                    if (count == 0) {
23                        toRemove.add(entry.getKey());
24                    } else {
25                        candidates.put(entry.getKey(), count);
26                    }
27                }
28                for (int key : toRemove) {
29                    candidates.remove(key);
30                }
31            }
32        }
33
34        // Phase 2: Verify candidates
35        Map<Integer, Integer> counts = new HashMap<>();
36        for (int num : nums) {
37            if (candidates.containsKey(num)) {
38                counts.put(num, counts.getOrDefault(num, 0) + 1);
39            }
40        }
41
42        List<Integer> result = new ArrayList<>();
43        for (Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> entry : counts.entrySet()) {
44            if (entry.getValue() > nums.length / k) {
45                result.add(entry.getKey());
46            }
47        }
48
49        return result;
50    }
51}

1-3) Count-Min Sketch - Frequency Estimation

Algorithm Overview:

  • Probabilistic data structure for frequency estimation
  • Uses d hash functions and w counters per hash
  • Query: return minimum count across all hash functions
  • Guarantee: Overestimates by at most εn with probability 1-δ
  • Parameters: w = ⌈e/ε⌉, d = ⌈ln(1/δ)⌉

Template:

1class CountMinSketch {
2    private int[][] count; // d × w matrix
3    private int d; // Number of hash functions
4    private int w; // Width of each row
5    private long[] hashA, hashB; // Hash parameters
6    private static final long PRIME = 2147483647L; // Large prime
7
8    /**
9     * Constructor
10     * epsilon: error tolerance (e.g., 0.01 for 1% error)
11     * delta: failure probability (e.g., 0.01 for 99% confidence)
12     */
13    public CountMinSketch(double epsilon, double delta) {
14        this.w = (int) Math.ceil(Math.E / epsilon);
15        this.d = (int) Math.ceil(Math.log(1.0 / delta));
16        this.count = new int[d][w];
17
18        // Initialize hash functions: h(x) = ((a*x + b) % p) % w
19        Random random = new Random();
20        hashA = new long[d];
21        hashB = new long[d];
22        for (int i = 0; i < d; i++) {
23            hashA[i] = random.nextInt(Integer.MAX_VALUE);
24            hashB[i] = random.nextInt(Integer.MAX_VALUE);
25        }
26    }
27
28    private int hash(int item, int i) {
29        // Hash function i
30        long hash = ((hashA[i] * item + hashB[i]) % PRIME) % w;
31        return (int) hash;
32    }
33
34    /**
35     * Add item to sketch
36     * time = O(d)
37     * space = O(1)
38     */
39    public void add(int item) {
40        for (int i = 0; i < d; i++) {
41            int index = hash(item, i);
42            count[i][index]++;
43        }
44    }
45
46    /**
47     * Estimate frequency of item
48     * time = O(d)
49     * space = O(1)
50     * Returns: estimated count (may overestimate, never underestimate)
51     */
52    public int estimateCount(int item) {
53        int minCount = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
54        for (int i = 0; i < d; i++) {
55            int index = hash(item, i);
56            minCount = Math.min(minCount, count[i][index]);
57        }
58        return minCount;
59    }
60
61    /**
62     * Get space usage
63     */
64    public int getSpace() {
65        return d * w; // Number of counters
66    }
67}

1# Python Count-Min Sketch
2import math
3import random
4
5class CountMinSketch:
6    def __init__(self, epsilon: float, delta: float):
7        """
8        epsilon: error tolerance
9        delta: failure probability
10        """
11        self.w = math.ceil(math.e / epsilon)
12        self.d = math.ceil(math.log(1.0 / delta))
13        self.count = [[0] * self.w for _ in range(self.d)]
14
15        # Initialize hash parameters
16        PRIME = 2147483647
17        self.hash_a = [random.randint(1, PRIME-1) for _ in range(self.d)]
18        self.hash_b = [random.randint(0, PRIME-1) for _ in range(self.d)]
19        self.PRIME = PRIME
20
21    def _hash(self, item: int, i: int) -> int:
22        """Hash function i"""
23        return ((self.hash_a[i] * item + self.hash_b[i]) % self.PRIME) % self.w
24
25    def add(self, item: int) -> None:
26        """Time: O(d), Space: O(1)"""
27        for i in range(self.d):
28            index = self._hash(item, i)
29            self.count[i][index] += 1
30
31    def estimate_count(self, item: int) -> int:
32        """Time: O(d), Returns estimated frequency"""
33        return min(self.count[i][self._hash(item, i)] for i in range(self.d))

Usage Example:

1// Track top-k frequent elements in stream
2CountMinSketch cms = new CountMinSketch(0.01, 0.01); // 1% error, 99% confidence
3
4// Process stream
5for (int item : stream) {
6    cms.add(item);
7}
8
9// Query frequencies
10int freq = cms.estimateCount(42); // Estimated frequency of item 42

1-4) Bloom Filter - Membership Testing

Algorithm Overview:

  • Bit array of m bits, k hash functions
  • Insert: Set k bits to 1 using k hash functions
  • Query: Check if all k bits are 1
  • Guarantee: No false negatives, false positive rate ≈ (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k
  • Optimal k: (m/n) × ln(2)

Template:

1class BloomFilter {
2    private BitSet bitSet;
3    private int m; // Bit array size
4    private int k; // Number of hash functions
5    private long[] hashSeeds;
6
7    /**
8     * Constructor
9     * expectedElements: expected number of elements
10     * falsePositiveRate: desired false positive probability (e.g., 0.01)
11     */
12    public BloomFilter(int expectedElements, double falsePositiveRate) {
13        // Optimal m: -(n * ln(p)) / (ln(2)^2)
14        this.m = (int) Math.ceil(
15            -(expectedElements * Math.log(falsePositiveRate)) / Math.pow(Math.log(2), 2)
16        );
17
18        // Optimal k: (m/n) * ln(2)
19        this.k = (int) Math.ceil((m / (double) expectedElements) * Math.log(2));
20
21        this.bitSet = new BitSet(m);
22
23        // Initialize hash seeds
24        Random random = new Random();
25        hashSeeds = new long[k];
26        for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
27            hashSeeds[i] = random.nextLong();
28        }
29    }
30
31    private int hash(String item, int i) {
32        long hash = hashSeeds[i];
33        for (char c : item.toCharArray()) {
34            hash = hash * 31 + c;
35        }
36        return Math.abs((int) (hash % m));
37    }
38
39    /**
40     * Add item to filter
41     * time = O(k)
42     * space = O(1)
43     */
44    public void add(String item) {
45        for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
46            int index = hash(item, i);
47            bitSet.set(index);
48        }
49    }
50
51    /**
52     * Check if item might be in set
53     * time = O(k)
54     * space = O(1)
55     * Returns: true if might exist (possible false positive)
56     *          false if definitely not in set (no false negative)
57     */
58    public boolean mightContain(String item) {
59        for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
60            int index = hash(item, i);
61            if (!bitSet.get(index)) {
62                return false; // Definitely not in set
63            }
64        }
65        return true; // Might be in set
66    }
67
68    /**
69     * Get expected false positive rate
70     */
71    public double getFalsePositiveRate(int insertedElements) {
72        return Math.pow(1 - Math.exp(-k * insertedElements / (double) m), k);
73    }
74}

1# Python Bloom Filter
2import math
3import mmh3  # MurmurHash3 (install: pip install mmh3)
4from bitarray import bitarray  # (install: pip install bitarray)
5
6class BloomFilter:
7    def __init__(self, expected_elements: int, false_positive_rate: float):
8        """
9        expected_elements: expected number of items
10        false_positive_rate: desired false positive probability
11        """
12        # Optimal m
13        self.m = math.ceil(
14            -(expected_elements * math.log(false_positive_rate)) / (math.log(2) ** 2)
15        )
16
17        # Optimal k
18        self.k = math.ceil((self.m / expected_elements) * math.log(2))
19
20        self.bit_array = bitarray(self.m)
21        self.bit_array.setall(0)
22
23    def add(self, item: str) -> None:
24        """Time: O(k)"""
25        for i in range(self.k):
26            index = mmh3.hash(item, i) % self.m
27            self.bit_array[index] = 1
28
29    def might_contain(self, item: str) -> bool:
30        """Time: O(k), False positives possible, no false negatives"""
31        for i in range(self.k):
32            index = mmh3.hash(item, i) % self.m
33            if not self.bit_array[index]:
34                return False  # Definitely not in set
35        return True  # Might be in set

Usage Example:

1// Check if URL has been visited
2BloomFilter bf = new BloomFilter(10000, 0.01); // 10k URLs, 1% false positive
3
4// Add URLs
5bf.add("https://example.com");
6bf.add("https://google.com");
7
8// Query
9if (bf.mightContain("https://example.com")) {
10    // Might be visited (99% confident)
11}
12
13if (!bf.mightContain("https://new-site.com")) {
14    // Definitely NOT visited (100% sure)
15}

2) LeetCode Problems by Pattern

2-1) Reservoir Sampling

Problem Difficulty Pattern Notes
LC 382 Medium Single random (k=1) Linked list, unknown length
LC 398 Medium Weighted sampling Array with duplicates
LC 528 Medium Weighted random Prefix sum + binary search
LC 519 Medium Random 2D matrix Reset and shuffle

LC 382 - Linked List Random Node

  • Unknown list length
  • O(n) time per call, O(1) space
  • Classic reservoir sampling k=1

LC 398 - Random Pick Index

  • Multiple occurrences of target
  • Uniform selection among duplicates
  • Reservoir sampling variant

2-2) Boyer-Moore Majority Vote

Problem Difficulty Threshold Candidates
LC 169 Easy > n/2 1 candidate
LC 229 Medium > n/3 2 candidates
- - > n/k k-1 candidates

LC 169 - Majority Element

  • Guaranteed majority exists
  • Single candidate suffices
  • No verification phase needed

LC 229 - Majority Element II

  • Up to 2 elements can appear > n/3 times
  • Must verify candidates
  • Two-phase algorithm essential

2-3) Count-Min Sketch (Conceptual)

Applications (Not direct LC problems):

  • Top K Frequent Elements (LC 347) - Can use CMS + min-heap
  • Kth Largest Element in Stream (LC 703) - Related streaming problem
  • Data Stream as Disjoint Intervals (LC 352) - Stream processing

When to Apply:

  • Large streams where HashMap exceeds memory
  • Approximate answers acceptable
  • Need frequency estimates, not exact counts

2-4) Bloom Filter (Conceptual)

Applications (Not direct LC problems):

  • Contains Duplicate (LC 217) - Can use BF for space optimization
  • First Unique Character (LC 387) - Variant: use BF as pre-filter
  • Design HashMap (LC 706) - Can add BF layer for optimization

When to Apply:

  • Membership queries on large sets
  • False positives acceptable
  • Space-critical applications (e.g., web crawlers)

2-5) Related Streaming Problems

Problem Difficulty Pattern Algorithm
LC 295 Hard Data stream median Two heaps
LC 346 Easy Moving average Sliding window + queue
LC 352 Hard Disjoint intervals TreeMap/BST
LC 703 Easy Kth largest in stream Min-heap

3) Common Mistakes & Edge Cases

🚫 Common Mistakes

Reservoir Sampling:

  1. Wrong Probability Calculation

    1// ❌ WRONG: This is NOT uniform
2if (random.nextInt(i + 1) < k) { // Incorrect for k=1
3    reservoir[...] = element;
4}
5
6// ✅ CORRECT: For k=1
7if (random.nextInt(count) == 0) {
8    result = element;
9}

  2. Forgetting to Update Count

    1// ❌ WRONG: Count not incremented
2while (cur != null) {
3    if (random.nextInt(count) == 0) result = cur.val;
4    cur = cur.next; // Forgot: count++
5}

  3. Off-by-One in Random Range

    1// ❌ WRONG: random.nextInt(count + 1) for decision
2// ✅ CORRECT: random.nextInt(count) for k=1

Boyer-Moore:

  1. Missing Verification Phase

    1// ❌ WRONG: Assuming candidate is always majority
2return candidate; // What if no majority exists?
3
4// ✅ CORRECT: Verify count
5int actualCount = 0;
6for (int num : nums) {
7    if (num == candidate) actualCount++;
8}
9return actualCount > nums.length / 2 ? candidate : -1;

  2. Wrong Cancellation Logic

    1// ❌ WRONG: Only decrement candidate's count
2if (num != candidate) count--;
3
4// ✅ CORRECT: Check count == 0 to reset
5if (count == 0) {
6    candidate = num;
7    count = 1;
8}

  3. For LC 229: Not Handling Same Candidates

    1// ❌ WRONG: candidate1 and candidate2 can be same initially
2
3// ✅ CORRECT: Check candidate1 first, then candidate2
4if (num == candidate1) count1++;
5else if (num == candidate2) count2++;

Count-Min Sketch:

  1. Using Max Instead of Min

    1// ❌ WRONG: Taking maximum (gives wrong estimate)
2int max = Arrays.stream(counts).max().getAsInt();
3
4// ✅ CORRECT: Taking minimum (minimizes overestimation)
5int min = Arrays.stream(counts).min().getAsInt();

  2. Insufficient Hash Functions

    • Too few hash functions → high collision rate
    • Rule of thumb: d ≥ 3 for reasonable accuracy

Bloom Filter:

  1. Resetting Bits on Delete

    1// ❌ WRONG: Bloom filters don't support deletion
2public void delete(String item) {
3    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
4        bitSet.clear(hash(item, i)); // Breaks other items!
5    }
6}
7
8// ✅ CORRECT: Use Counting Bloom Filter for deletions

  2. Ignoring False Positive Rate

    • Too small bit array → high false positive rate
    • Calculate optimal m and k based on expected elements

⚠️ Edge Cases

  1. Empty Stream

    • Reservoir: Return empty or throw exception
    • Boyer-Moore: No majority element
    • Bloom Filter: All queries return false

  2. Single Element

    • Reservoir: Always return that element
    • Boyer-Moore: Single element is majority

  3. All Elements Same

    • Boyer-Moore: Single candidate with count = n
    • Reservoir: Any element is representative

  4. Stream Size Unknown

    • Reservoir Sampling handles this naturally
    • Count-Min Sketch: May need to adjust parameters dynamically

  5. Integer Overflow

    • Count-Min Sketch: Use long for large counts
    • Hash functions: Use proper modulo arithmetic

4) Interview Tips & Complexity Analysis

💡 Interview Strategy

When to Use Streaming Algorithms:

Recognition Patterns:

  1. “Process data stream” → Streaming algorithm
  2. “Limited memory” or “O(1) space” → Streaming
  3. “Unknown size” or “infinite stream” → Reservoir Sampling
  4. “Majority element” → Boyer-Moore
  5. “Approximate count/frequency” → Count-Min Sketch
  6. “Have you seen before?” → Bloom Filter

Problem-Solving Framework:

11. Identify constraint: Memory vs Accuracy
2   ├─ Exact answer needed? → Reservoir Sampling or Boyer-Moore
3   └─ Approximate OK? → Count-Min Sketch or Bloom Filter
4
52. Determine stream characteristics:
6   ├─ Need random samples? → Reservoir Sampling
7   ├─ Need heavy hitters? → Boyer-Moore
8   ├─ Need frequency estimates? → Count-Min Sketch
9   └─ Need membership tests? → Bloom Filter
10
113. Consider space-time tradeoff:
12   ├─ More space → Better accuracy
13   └─ Less space → More approximation error

📊 Complexity Analysis

Algorithm Time (per element) Space Accuracy
Reservoir Sampling O(1) O(k) Exact
Boyer-Moore O(1) O(k) Exact (if exists)
Count-Min Sketch O(d) O(w × d) ε-approximate
Bloom Filter O(k) O(m) bits False positives

Space Comparisons:

  • HashMap: O(n) - exact counts for all elements
  • Count-Min Sketch: O(w × d) where w × d << n
  • Bloom Filter: O(m) bits, m = O(n log(1/p))

Typical Parameters:

  • Count-Min Sketch: w = 1000, d = 5 → 5KB for millions of elements
  • Bloom Filter: 10 bits per element for 1% false positive rate

🎯 Interview Talking Points

  1. Why Reservoir Sampling Works:

    • “Each element has equal probability k/n of being selected”
    • “Prove by induction: P(element i selected) = k/i × (i/(i+1)) × … × (n-1)/n = k/n”

  2. Boyer-Moore Intuition:

    • “Pair up different elements and cancel them”
    • “Majority element survives cancellation”
    • “At most k-1 elements can appear more than n/k times”

  3. Count-Min Sketch Trade-off:

    • “Trade space for accuracy: w and d control error bounds”
    • “Never underestimates (only overestimates due to collisions)”
    • “Minimum across hash functions reduces overestimation”

  4. Bloom Filter Properties:

    • “False positives possible, false negatives impossible”
    • “Can’t delete items (unless using Counting Bloom Filter)”
    • “Space-efficient: ~10 bits per element for 1% FP rate”

🔧 Optimization Techniques

  1. Reservoir Sampling:

    • Use random.nextInt(count++) idiom for cleaner code
    • For weighted sampling: Use prefix sum + binary search

  2. Boyer-Moore:

    • For k=2 (majority > n/2): Skip verification if guaranteed
    • For k=3 (> n/3): Track exactly 2 candidates
    • For general k: Use HashMap with size k-1

  3. Count-Min Sketch:

    • Conservative Update: Only increment minimum count
    • Use faster hash functions (MurmurHash, CityHash)
    • Adjust w and d based on accuracy requirements

  4. Bloom Filter:

    • Choose hash functions that minimize correlation
    • Consider Counting Bloom Filter for deletions
    • Use bit-level operations for space efficiency

📚 Related Topics

  • Sampling: Stratified sampling, importance sampling
  • Approximate Algorithms: HyperLogLog (cardinality estimation)
  • Probabilistic: Skip lists, treaps
  • Streaming: Misra-Gries algorithm, Space Saving algorithm
  • Online Algorithms: Competitive analysis, adversarial models

5) Complete Code Examples

Example 1: Weighted Reservoir Sampling (LC 528)

1// LC 528 - Random Pick with Weight
2class Solution {
3    private int[] prefixSum;
4    private Random random;
5
6    /**
7     * time = O(N) for constructor
8     * space = O(N)
9     */
10    public Solution(int[] w) {
11        prefixSum = new int[w.length];
12        prefixSum[0] = w[0];
13        for (int i = 1; i < w.length; i++) {
14            prefixSum[i] = prefixSum[i - 1] + w[i];
15        }
16        random = new Random();
17    }
18
19    /**
20     * time = O(log N) using binary search
21     * space = O(1)
22     */
23    public int pickIndex() {
24        int target = random.nextInt(prefixSum[prefixSum.length - 1]) + 1;
25        // Binary search for target in prefixSum
26        int left = 0, right = prefixSum.length - 1;
27        while (left < right) {
28            int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
29            if (prefixSum[mid] < target) {
30                left = mid + 1;
31            } else {
32                right = mid;
33            }
34        }
35        return left;
36    }
37}

Example 2: Stream Median (Related Problem LC 295)

1// LC 295 - Find Median from Data Stream
2class MedianFinder {
3    private PriorityQueue<Integer> maxHeap; // Lower half
4    private PriorityQueue<Integer> minHeap; // Upper half
5
6    public MedianFinder() {
7        maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> b - a);
8        minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>();
9    }
10
11    /**
12     * time = O(log N)
13     * space = O(N)
14     */
15    public void addNum(int num) {
16        // Add to max heap first
17        maxHeap.offer(num);
18
19        // Balance: move largest from max to min
20        minHeap.offer(maxHeap.poll());
21
22        // Maintain size property: maxHeap.size >= minHeap.size
23        if (maxHeap.size() < minHeap.size()) {
24            maxHeap.offer(minHeap.poll());
25        }
26    }
27
28    /**
29     * time = O(1)
30     * space = O(1)
31     */
32    public double findMedian() {
33        if (maxHeap.size() > minHeap.size()) {
34            return maxHeap.peek();
35        }
36        return (maxHeap.peek() + minHeap.peek()) / 2.0;
37    }
38}

Summary

Core Streaming Algorithms:

  1. ✅ Reservoir Sampling - Uniform random sampling from stream
  2. ✅ Boyer-Moore - Find majority/heavy hitters (exact)
  3. ✅ Count-Min Sketch - Frequency estimation (approximate)
  4. ✅ Bloom Filter - Membership testing (probabilistic)

Key Takeaways:

  • Streaming algorithms trade accuracy for space efficiency
  • Reservoir Sampling: Exact, O(k) space for k samples
  • Boyer-Moore: Exact for heavy hitters, O(k) space
  • Count-Min Sketch: Approximate, tunable error bounds
  • Bloom Filter: No false negatives, space-efficient

Interview Focus:

  • Understand probability proofs (especially Reservoir Sampling)
  • Know when to use each algorithm
  • Master two-phase verification (Boyer-Moore)
  • Explain space-accuracy tradeoffs

Practice Problems:

  • Start with LC 382, 398 (Reservoir Sampling)
  • Master LC 169, 229 (Boyer-Moore)
  • Understand LC 295, 703 (Stream + Heap)
  • Study hash-based designs (LC 706, 705)
← Back to Cheat Sheets Edit on GitHub →
← Stock TradingString →

CS_basics - Computer Science Fundamentals & Interview Preparation

GitHub • Documentation • Report Issues
